我们生活在一个奇怪的时代，他们在8月份变得更加陌生，当时美国总统公开支持被称为QAnon的边缘政治意识形态。随后，特朗普在接受福克斯新闻(Fox News)劳拉·英格拉汉姆(Laura Ingraham)采访时加倍努力，重复了QAnon的谈话要点，比如“富人”如何为美国城市的抗议活动提供资金，以及他的民主党竞争对手乔·拜登(Joe Biden)是如何被“你从未听说过的人”控制的。那些生活在黑暗阴影中的人。“。即使是英格拉汉姆，通常是总统的坚定支持者，似乎也大吃一惊。“那是什么意思？”她插嘴说。“这听起来像是阴谋论。”
QAnon是一种阴谋论，但它也是许多其他东西，依次是在线巨魔运动，一种救世主的世界观，一种互动角色扮演的形式，以及一种销售T恤的方式。对于它的批评者来说，QAnon是一个危险地失控的骗局；对于真正的信徒来说，它是一种包罗万象的生活方式。在不断演变的信条拼贴中，这一理论认为，一个由民主党高级政客和媒体精英组成的撒旦阴谋集团正在运营一个儿童性交易团伙，同时密谋控制美国政府和社会。根据QAnon的说法，在这个正在进行的深度国家计划和敬畏上帝的公民之间，唯一的障碍就是唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)的奇特存在，他的每一条推文和政治噱头实际上都是对邪恶势力的绝密影子运动的掩护。
QAnon听起来像Z级恐怖电影的情节，但它是互联网的产物，更具体地说，是社交媒体网络的产物。它的起源可以追溯到2017年10月下旬，当时在特别顾问罗伯特·穆勒(Robert Mueller)调查俄罗斯干预2016年大选期间，出现了一篇匿名帖子。张贴在4chan网站的“政治不正确”论坛上的文本部分写道，“人权理事会的引渡已于昨天与几个国家生效，以防越境越境。护照获准加盖标记，自10/30@12：01起生效。预计挑衅组织的大规模骚乱和其他逃离美国的骚乱将会发生。当NG启动时，美国海军陆战队将进行行动。“。由于它对希拉里·克林顿(Hillary Clinton)的执着，对国内动乱的痴迷，以及希望军方介入美国的文化战争，这封信几乎消失在一个论坛的背景噪音中，这个论坛已经在嗡嗡作响，其他自称是内部人士的人也在分发类似的保守派谈话要点。
然而，在接下来的几周里，从这个特殊的词沙拉中出现了一个奇怪的故事。在一系列同样隐晦的后续帖子中，发起人暗示他们是一名拥有绝密“Q许可”的政府内部人士，据称是从州内深处向网上的爱国者提供信息。“我们政府中的许多人崇拜撒旦，”一份新闻稿声称；其他人则包括圣经诗句的片段。所有令人震惊的预测都没有成真，也没有任何证明海报资质的证据出现。然而，这些帖子被渴望新内容的右翼YouTube人物放大了，“QAnon”在有阴谋论倾向的人中成了一个模因。4chan上出现了专门讨论这个话题的帖子，然后转移到8chan，然后通过Reddit、Facebook和Twitter悄悄进入主流。
到目前为止，已经有超过4700个Q值下降，正如狂热分子所说的那样。信徒们继续将Q令人费解的报道与新闻头条相匹配，浏览特朗普的言论，寻找他们所说的风暴的提法：一场内战，在这场战争中，诡计多端的精英们将得到应得的惩罚，美国将再次伟大起来。部分得益于YouTube和Facebook上建议算法的强大功能，这种偏执的观点已经从边缘的幻想滚雪球般发展成为一场可以被合法描述为运动的东西。
已故的英国历史学家诺曼·科恩(Norman Cohn)在1957年发表的关于末世论信仰体系的论文“千年追求”(The Purchase Of The Millennium)中，审视了“千禧年主义”--他坚信救世主会降临，惩罚邪恶，并将世界重塑为信徒的天堂。他主要专注于中世纪的欧洲，是首批量化受社会变革和经济不平等困扰的人群如何特别容易受到末日阴谋论影响的学者之一。无能为力会招致愤怒；愤怒会寻找替罪羊；当事实证明太不方便或情况太复杂而无法分析时，幻想就会弥合现实和想象之间的鸿沟。科恩写道，承诺世界末日解救的末日预言者一直与我们在一起，但“如果威胁足够压倒性，迷失方向足够广泛和严重，可能会出现最具爆炸性的大规模错觉。”科恩认为，正是这种错觉为人类历史上从十字军东征到纳粹主义的黑暗时期铺平了道路。
您可能会认为，如果有一个
西村将该网站的名字取自第二频道：在模拟时代，视频游戏机已经连接到的一个空荡荡的电视乐队，对于该网站最早的采用者极客来说，这是一个一目了然的笑话。2channel故意看起来粗糙，完全基于文本，成年人完全无法理解，它被设计成一个寻找自己部落的地方。它很快成为传统大众媒体的替代品：在这个空间里，青少年和20多岁的人在网上花费越来越多的空闲时间，可以交换信息和发泄沮丧，而不用担心报复。到2002年，2channel也是越来越多直言不讳的少数用户的家园，他们将对社会的愤怒转化为军国主义爱国主义和种族优越感的表达，兴高采烈地否认战时暴行，同时攻击任何不同意他们阴谋论偏执的人。在他们看来，他们国家的麻烦完全归咎于自由派政客和政治正确的主流媒体，他们认为，这些人故意贬低日本的伟大，以讨好新崛起的朝鲜和中国。这个不断受到委屈的网络社区后来被称为Netto-Uyoku(“网络右翼”)。
Qanon出现在4chan上并不是巧合，4chan是一个匿名的在线公告牌，是日本2Channel的直系后代。(4chan于2003年推出，基于名为Futaba Channel的双频道衍生产品使用的代码。)。最早的4chan参与者聚集在一起，是出于对日本奇幻的热爱，就像视频游戏、卡通和色情作品中所展示的那样。但是，在该网站成立后的几年里，其匿名用户在不知不觉中重蹈了2Channel前辈的覆辙。开始时的跨文化庆祝活动演变成恐同、民族主义、反女权主义和白人至上主义。到2015年，4chan已经有了自己版本的日本净右翼-另类右翼-以及自己的阴谋论，形式是被称为Gamergate的厌女症巨魔运动。紧随其后的是披萨门(Pizzagate)，2016年，维基解密(WikiLeaks)对希拉里·克林顿(Hillary Clinton)竞选团队主席约翰·波德斯塔(John Podesta)被黑客攻击的电子邮件进行了黑暗的幻想。从这些信件中，信徒们编造了一个恐怖的故事：民主党高级官员在华盛顿特区的一家披萨店外经营着一个儿童性爱团伙。另类右翼人士以及从亚历克斯·琼斯(Alex Jones)到过渡团队成员小迈克尔·弗林(Michael Flynn Jr.)等特朗普的支持者放大了这个故事。琼斯后来放弃了，弗林很快就被赶了出去，但即使是官方批准的短暂气味也有助于刺激披萨门进一步演变为QAnon运动。
Qanon似乎代表了美国人独特的愤怒表达方式，这是由我们历史上这一特殊时刻塑造的。但它的剧本的第一页是在日本写的，这是日本年轻人在社会政治混乱时期打造的平台的产物。在今天的动荡时期，这个国家的历史似乎正在重演：2011年福岛核灾难期间，网络巨魔错误地声称，中国抢劫者正在袭击疏散人员被遗弃的家园，这引发了一股右翼义务警员的热潮，他们保卫那些后来被证明是空城的地方。今年夏天，美国也出现了类似的情况，当时社交媒体的谣言促使小城镇的居民拿起武器，反对据称满载安提法抗议者的大巴，这些抗议者从未到达。同样，在9月份，Facebook助长了关于不存在安提法纵火犯的谣言，导致一些俄勒冈州居民在肆虐的野火中藐视疏散命令。
阴谋论在艰难时期蓬勃发展，但今天，我们超级互联的社区的工具-互联网，无时无刻不在我们的家庭和智能手机中；海量社交媒体网络；以及算法推荐系统-以既赋予我们权力又有害的方式将我们联系在一起，为阴谋论提供了超强动力。事实上，这些技术以甚至它们的创造者似乎都很难面对的方式助长了冲突：看看Facebook在其所有服务中持续未能遏制极端组织的情况就知道了。QAnon可能是第一个成为近乎宗教的网络阴谋-从网络的黑暗边缘延伸到母亲们的社区团体-但肯定不会是最后一个。就像日本和美国这样截然不同的后工业信息社会反复斗争所表明的那样，人性实际上保证了这一点。
note_en:
We live in strange times, and they grew stranger still in August, when the  President of the United States publicly supported the fringe political ideology known as QAnon. Trump then doubled down during an interview with Laura Ingraham, on Fox News, repeating QAnon talking points, such as how “rich people” were bankrolling protests in American cities and how his Democratic rival,  Joe Biden, was being controlled by “people that you’ve never heard of. People that are in the dark shadows.” Even Ingraham, normally an unflappable supporter of the President, seemed taken aback. “What does that mean?” she interjected. “That sounds like a conspiracy theory.”
  QAnon is a  conspiracy theory, but it is many other things as well, by turns an online troll campaign, a Messianic world view, a form of interactive role-playing, and a way to sell T-shirts. For its critics, QAnon is a hoax spun dangerously out of control; for true believers, it is an all-encompassing life style. The theory holds, among an ever-evolving collage of tenets, that a satanic cabal of high-ranking Democratic politicians and members of the media élite is running a child sex-trafficking ring while plotting to take control of American government and society. According to QAnon lore, the only thing standing between this ongoing deep-state scheme and God-fearing citizens is the singular presence of Donald Trump, whose every tweet and political stunt is actually cover for a top-secret shadow campaign against the forces of evil.
 QAnon sounds like the plot of a Z-grade horror movie, but it is a product of the Internet and, more specifically, of social-media networks. Its origins can be traced to late October, 2017, when an anonymous post appeared during the special counsel Robert Mueller’s investigation of Russian interference in the 2016 elections. Posted on the “politically incorrect” forum of the Web site 4chan, the text read, in part, “HRC extradition already in motion effective yesterday with several countries in case of cross border run. Passport approved to be flagged effective 10/30 @ 12:01am. Expect massive riots organized in defiance and others fleeing the US to occur. US M’s will conduct the operation while NG activated.” With its fixation on Hillary Clinton, its obsession with civil unrest, and its hopes for the military to intervene in the nation’s culture wars, the missive virtually disappeared into the background noise of a forum already buzzing with other self-described insiders dispensing similar conservative talking points.
  Yet over the next few weeks, a strange narrative emerged from this particular word salad. In a series of equally cryptic follow-up posts, the originator hinted that they were a government insider with top-secret “Q clearance,” supposedly feeding information from inside the deep state to patriots online. “Many in our govt worship Satan,” one dispatch claimed; others included snippets of Bible verses. None of the outrageous predictions came true, nor did any evidence for the poster’s credentials ever materialize. Nevertheless, the posts were amplified by right-wing YouTube personalities hungry for new content, and “QAnon” exploded as a meme among the conspiratorially inclined. Threads dedicated to the topic emerged on 4chan, moved to 8chan, then crept into the mainstream via Reddit, Facebook, and Twitter.
 To date, there have been more than forty-seven hundred Q drops, as aficionados call the posts. Devotees continue to match Q’s puzzling dispatches to news headlines and scan Trump’s remarks for references to what they call the Storm: a civil war in which the scheming élites will get their richly deserved comeuppance and America will be made great again. Thanks in part to the power of suggestion algorithms on YouTube and Facebook, this paranoid perspective has snowballed from a fringe fantasy into what could legitimately be described as a movement.
  In his 1957 treatise on eschatological belief systems, “ The Pursuit of the Millennium,” the late British historian Norman Cohn examined “millennialism”—the conviction that a savior will arrive to punish evil and refashion the world into a paradise for the faithful. Focussing mainly on medieval Europe, he was one of the first academics to quantify how populations beset by social change and economic inequality are uniquely susceptible to end-of-times conspiracy theories. Powerlessness begets rage; rage seeks a scapegoat; and, when facts prove too inconvenient or the situation too complicated to parse, fantasy bridges the gap between what is real and what is imaginary. Doomsayers promising apocalyptic deliverance have always been with us, Cohn wrote, but “if the threat was sufficiently overwhelming, the disorientation sufficiently widespread and acute, there could arise a mass delusion of the most explosive kind.” It is such delusions, Cohn believed, that paved the way for dark episodes in human history, ranging from the Crusades to Nazism.
  You might suppose that, with all the information now available to us, millennialist thinking would be in decline rather than running rampant online. But the warning signs were present from the early years of the Internet: a conspiracy similar to QAnon emerged in Japan in the nineties, offering a prescient glimpse of how the “information superhighway” would not lead to the rational utopia that its supporters had prophesied. After a decade of growth in the eighties, Japan unexpectedly plummeted into a prolonged economic recession and stagnation that lasted for the entirety of the nineties and two-thousands. That dark period, known as the Lost Decades, mirrors the state of affairs in post-Great Recession America: an economic superpower brought to its knees through the venality of C.E.O.s, bankers, and politicians; a lost generation of young people unable to leave the nest, deprived of careers or fulfilling employment; a profound ambivalence about what the future might hold. It was during the Lost Decades that a doomsday cult by the name of Aum Shinrikyo (Supreme Truth) flourished in Japan. Its conspiratorial outlook, influenced in equal measure by anime, Far Eastern philosophy, video games, and dystopian science fiction, was tailored to appeal to intellectually curious, socially alienated young citizens. By early 1995, Aum had managed to amass enough true believers to engineer a terrorist attack on the Tokyo subway system. Eager to hasten the advent of Armageddon, which they imagined would scour the planet of all but the faithful, cult members boarded trains and released a nerve gas during the morning rush hour. It killed thirteen people and injured thousands more.
 Another breed of intellectually curious and socially alienated young citizens would emerge on a platform that could be considered Japan’s last megahit of the twentieth century: the Web site 2channel. A college student named Hiroyuki Nishimura created the anonymous online bulletin-board site during a transformative moment in Japanese society. The telecom DoCoMo had launched the world’s first truly popular mobile Internet service in early 1999, fast-tracking Web access for millions of Japanese youth via cell phones. As a result, Japan—and Japanese women in particular—pioneered new forms of communication that the world now takes for granted, from non-stop texting and selfie-taking to the use of emojis. But Japan’s rapid Internet-ization would also have negative consequences, as became clear on 2channel.
 Nishimura took the name for the site from channel 2: the empty television band to which video-game consoles had been connected in the analog era, an instantly recognizable in-joke to the geeks who were the site’s earliest adopters. Deliberately crude-looking, entirely text-based, and utterly impenetrable to grownups, 2channel was designed as a place to find one’s tribe. It quickly emerged as an alternative to traditional mass media: a space where teen-agers and twentysomethings who were spending increasing amounts of their free time online could swap information and vent frustrations without fear of reprisal. By 2002, 2channel was also home to an increasingly vocal minority of users who funnelled their anger at society into expressions of militaristic patriotism and racial superiority, gleefully denying wartime atrocities while troll-brigading anyone who disagreed with their conspiratorial paranoia. In their eyes, the blame for their nation’s troubles fell squarely on liberal politicians and the politically correct mainstream media, who they believed had deliberately minimized Japan’s greatness to curry favor with the newly ascendant Korea and China. This perpetually aggrieved cyber community came to be known as the  netto-uyoku (the “net-right”).
  It is no coincidence that QAnon emerged on 4chan, an anonymous online bulletin board that is a direct descendant of Japan’s 2channel. (Launched in 2003, 4chan was based on the code used by a 2channel spinoff called Futaba Channel.) The earliest 4chan participants gathered out of a love for Japanese fantasy, as exhibited in video games, cartoons, and pornography. But, in the years after the site’s founding, its anonymous users unwittingly retraced the steps of their predecessors on 2channel. What had begun as a cross-cultural celebration devolved into homophobia, nationalism, anti-feminism, and white supremacy. By 2015, 4chan had its own version of Japan’s net-right—the alt-right—and its own breed of conspiracy theory, in the form of the misogynist troll campaign known as  Gamergate. This was quickly followed by  Pizzagate, in 2016, a dark fantasy spun out of the dump, by WikiLeaks, of the hacked e-mails of  John Podesta, Hillary Clinton’s campaign chairman. From those missives, believers wove a horror story: high-ranking Democratic officials were running a child sex ring out of a pizza parlor in Washington, D.C. The alt-right, and Trump supporters ranging from Alex Jones to the transition-team member Michael Flynn, Jr., amplified the tale. Jones later recanted, and Flynn was quickly forced out, but even the brief whiff of an official imprimatur helped spur Pizzagate’s further evolution into the QAnon movement.
 QAnon may seem to represent a uniquely American expression of rage, shaped by this peculiar moment in our history. But the first pages of its playbook were written in Japan, a product of the platforms forged by Japanese youths in a period of socio-political dislocation. In today’s troubled times, that nation’s history seems to be repeating itself: during the Fukushima nuclear disaster, in 2011, net trolls falsely claimed that Chinese looters were descending upon the abandoned homes of evacuees, inspiring a rush of right-wing vigilantes to defend what turned out to be empty towns. A comparable scene played out in the U.S., this summer, when social-media rumors prompted residents of small towns to take up arms against purported busloads of Antifa protesters that never arrived. So, too, in September, when  Facebook-fuelled rumors of nonexistent Antifa arsonists led a number of Oregon residents to defy evacuation orders amid raging wildfires.
 Conspiracy theories blossom in trying times, but today they are supercharged by the tools of our hyperconnected communities—the Internet, ever-present in our homes and smartphones; massive social-media networks; and algorithmic recommendation systems that connect us in ways both empowering and toxic. Indeed, these technologies promote strife in ways that even their creators seem hard-pressed to confront: witness Facebook’s ongoing failure to rein in extremist groups across all its services. QAnon may be the first online conspiracy to become  a near-religion—with a surprising pull that extends from the dark fringes of the Web to neighborhood groups for moms—but it certainly won’t be the last. As illustrated by the recurring struggles of post-industrial information societies as disparate as Japan and America, human nature virtually guarantees it.
title: Qanon的闪烁警告

提供智能家居健身设备的中国初创公司Fiture在腾讯牵头、天使轮投资者红杉资本中国(Sequoia Capital China)参与的首轮融资中获得了6500万美元。
贝塔斯曼亚洲投资公司(BAI)，德国贝塔斯曼集团专注于亚洲的投资部门，由香港亿万富翁阿德里安·郑志刚领导的投资公司C Ventures，以及中国跨境投资平台国泰资本，在周五的一份声明中表示，贝塔斯曼亚洲投资公司(BAI)参与了首轮融资。
其他投资者包括总部位于中国的BA Capital，该公司专门押注消费品牌和产品；中信另类资产管理公司CPE；以及投资于大中华区和全球互联网和消费行业的All-Stars Investment。
白声称这轮6500万美元的融资是全球健身科技初创公司中规模最大的首轮融资，大约四个月前，随着中国健康意识的提高和消费者行为从健身房锻炼转向家庭健身解决方案，为中国2亿用户提供服务的健身应用Keep达到了10亿美元的独角兽估值。
根据行业研究机构ibaogao的数据，Keep在中国占据主导地位，2019年的市场份额估计为87.7%。该公司也得到了腾讯的支持，在5月份由专注于中国的成长期投资公司Jeneration Capital Management牵头的E系列融资中筹集了8,000万美元。
总部位于成都的Fiture专注于智能家居健身设备的开发，计划通过该设备提供互动健身课程、运动数据跟踪、定制培训计划和在线用户社区。
该公司的网站显示，该公司将很快推出其首款产品Fiture Mojing(中文字面意思是“魔镜”)，这是一款智能健身产品，类似于总部位于加州的Tempo的一体式数字健身屏幕Tempo Studio。
该公司联合创始人兼首席执行官唐天光表示，新资金将帮助Fiture继续投资研发，为用户提供更具互动性的健身内容。
BAI董事总经理威尔·王天凡在声明中表示：“利用我们在欧洲和美洲的媒体和服务网络，BAI致力于投资和帮助中国品牌的国际化，特别是他们在欧洲市场的扩张-欧洲市场正日益成为他们拥有新增长机会的首选市场。”
note_en:
FITURE, a Chinese startup that offers smart home fitness equipment, has secured $65 million in a Series A round of financing led by Tencent with participation from its angel round investor Sequoia Capital China.
 Bertelsmann Asia Investments (BAI), the Asia-focused investment arm of German conglomerate Bertelsmann; C Ventures, an investment firm led by Hong Kong billionaire Adrian Cheng Chi-kong; and Chinese cross-border investment platform  Cathay Capital, participated in the Series A round, said BAI in a statement on Friday.
 Other investors included China-based  BA Capital, which exclusively bets on consumer brands and products; CITIC’s alternative asset manager CPE; and All-Stars Investment, which invests in the internet and consumer sectors in Greater China and worldwide.
 The $65 million round, which BAI claims to be the largest Series A round in a fitness tech startup globally, came about four months after Keep, a fitness app that serves 200 million users in China, reached a unicorn valuation of $1 billion amid China’s rising health awareness and a shift in consumer behaviour from gym workouts to at-home fitness solutions.
 Keep is a dominant player in China and had an estimated market share of 87.7 per cent in 2019, according to industry researcher ibaogao.com. The firm, also backed by Tencent,  had raised $80 million in a Series E round led by China-focused, growth-stage investment firm Jeneration Capital Management in May.
 Chengdu-based FITURE focuses on the development of smart home fitness equipment, through which it plans to offer interactive fitness courses, exercise data tracking, customised training plans, and an online user community.
 Its website shows that the firm will soon launch its debut product, FITURE Mojing (which literally means “magic mirror” in Chinese), a smart fitness product similar to California-based Tempo’s all-in-one digital workout screen The Tempo Studio.
  The new funding would help FITURE continue investing in R&D to provide users with more interactive fitness content, said the company co-founder and CEO Tang Tianguang.
 “Leveraging our media and service network in Europe and Americas, BAI is committed to investing in and assisting the internationalization of Chinese brands, especially their expansion into the European market – which is increasingly becoming their go-to market with new growth opportunities, ” said Will Wang Tianfan, managing director of BAI, in the statement.
title: 提供智能家居健身设备的中国初创公司Fiture首次融资6500万美元，腾讯领投
title_en: Fiture, a Chinese startup that offers smart home fitness equipment, raises $65M Series A led by Tencent

一位大约2400年前去世的战士上半身的一块纹身皮肤将成为大英博物馆9月份开幕的展览中最不寻常的展品之一。
这次展览将让人们了解Scythians-凶猛的游牧骑手，他们统治着一个从黑海横跨西伯利亚到中国边境长达1000年的帝国。在西方鲜为人知，他们被视为所有俄罗斯人祖先的一部分。
这张皮肤将是从圣彼得堡隐士博物馆借来的数百件物品中的一件。许多都是第一次离开俄罗斯，其中一些是最近几年才挖掘出来的。木头、皮肤、骨头、纺织品甚至食物-比如一个皮包里仍然发现了两块奶酪-之所以被非凡地保存下来，是因为西伯利亚人在西伯利亚永久冻土深处挖掘了他们的坟墓。随着气候变化融化冰冻的土地，许多地点正在进行抢救性挖掘，冒着摧毁这个仍然神秘的文明的珍宝的风险。
馆长圣·约翰·辛普森说：“在这个博物馆里，我们熟悉那些建造城市、生活在建筑环境中、写下自己的历史的人们。”…。西提亚人没有书面语言，因此没有留下任何关于他们自己的记录，作为游牧牧民，他们除了自己的坟墓之外，没有建造任何永久的东西，对我们来说，幸运的是，他们用生活中拥有的一切填满了坟墓。这些坟墓就是他们的纪念碑。“。
在最近英国和俄罗斯外交关系降温的整个过程中，博物馆一直与赫米蒂奇保持着友好的关系，并在2014年引发了争议，当时它借给圣彼得堡博物馆一颗竞争激烈的帕台农神庙大理石，这是自19世纪埃尔金勋爵(Lord Elgin)将这些大理石从雅典带回英国以来，博物馆首次离开英国。从赫米蒂奇博物馆和哈萨克斯坦国家博物馆借来的贷款，使这次展览成为可能，展示了伦敦博物馆缺乏材料的一种文化。
关于西提亚人的文字记载，高超的骑手，杰出的弓箭手和专业的手艺人，都来自于他们敬畏的、经常受到惊吓的邻居。希腊历史学家希罗多德的描述包括声称，战士们在喝了他们的血后，用战斗中受害者的头皮做了斗篷。这些描述如此生动，以至于辛普森认为希罗多德一定是在黑海上的希腊定居点度过了一段时间，会见了来自他们领土的锡西亚人和旅行者。
人们一直质疑希罗多德是第一个历史学家还是第一个说谎者，但越来越多的考古学正在证明他的叙述的准确性。
馆长认为，有一种习俗解释了锡西亚人是如何应对帝国大部分地区的恶劣气候的。希罗多德描述了他们是如何拿起大麻种子，把它们扔在毛毡帐篷里的红色滚烫的石头上，呼吸蒸汽，然后“高兴地喊出声来”。最近的发掘发现了与描述完全相同的种子、火盆、三脚架和石头，展览将重现其中一个大麻燃烧器-尽管没有令人陶醉的烟雾。
许多贷款反映了锡西亚人的游牧生活方式，包括一张巧妙的可折叠桌子，辛普森将其描述为“原始宜家扁平背包”。他们的牛群为他们提供肉类、牛奶和奶酪，如在坟墓中发现的碎片，这些碎片可能是用绵羊、山羊或马奶制成的，并打算作为战士来世的食物。
贷款中将有一个金腰带扣，上面画着一群人在树下哀悼一名死去的战士。他的马看起来也很悲伤，这是有充分理由的：发掘结果显示，它们被宰杀的骏马伴随着斯基西亚人走向坟墓。它来自彼得大帝时期最早有记录的墓穴发掘之一，并成为他的新博物馆的第一件物品。
另一个可能是由希腊工匠制作的皮带扣上，画着一位留着胡须的战士，头发飘逸，手持长矛，赤背骑马。他穿着柔软的鞋子和长长的刺绣长袍，辛普森认为这一点很重要：正是他们保存下来的身体证明了西塞梯的男男女女身上有很多纹身，但没有外人描述他们。身体显示，胸口、上臂和腿上有抽象图案或时髦动物的纹身，外人是看不到的。
即将参展的皮肤上的纹身显示了一只有着凶猛爪子的老虎的一部分。它是在帕兹里克的一个坟墓中发现的，帕兹里克是西伯利亚阿拉泰山脉联合国教科文组织世界遗产的一部分，墓地里的物品包括世界上已知最古老的地毯，以及五个身上有大量纹身的人的身体。现在冷藏卡车待命，以防发现人或动物的遗骸，比如2007年发现的一名身穿貂皮大衣的酋长。
辛普森说，当帕兹里克墓穴在20世纪40年代被挖掘时，他们没有办法保存整个身体，所以务实地砍下了头部和任何特别有趣的身体部位。
大英博物馆只收藏了很少的与西塞梯人有关的物品，大多记录了他们与邻近帝国的接触。一块泥板上记录着，公元前7世纪，亚述国王以萨哈顿同意派一位亚述公主嫁给锡西亚领导人巴塔鲁亚，以签署一项和平条约。辛普森说：“条约显然生效了，但她用在亚述宫殿里的生活换来了游牧民族的毛毡帐篷--人们非常希望听到她的说法，但我们再也听不到她的消息了。”
note_en:
A piece of tattooed skin from the upper torso of a warrior who died about 2,400 years ago will be one of the more unusual items on display as part of a  British Museum exhibition which opens in September.
 The exhibition will shine a light on the Scythians – fierce nomadic horsemen who ruled an empire stretching from the Black Sea across Siberia to the borders of China for 1,000 years. Little known in the west, they are regarded as part of the ancestry of all Russians.
  The skin will be among hundreds of objects on loan from the Hermitage Museum in St Petersburg. Many are leaving Russia for the first time, including some excavated only in the last few years. The extraordinary preservation of wood, skin, bone, textiles and even food – such as a leather bag found still containing two pieces of cheese – was because the Scythians dug their tombs deep into the Siberian permafrost. Rescue excavations are now underway at many sites as climate change thaws the frozen ground, risking the destruction of treasures from a still mysterious civilisation.
  Curator St John Simpson said: “Mostly in this museum we are familiar with peoples who built cities, lived in a built environment, and wrote their own histories … The Scythians had no written language and so left no accounts of themselves, and as nomadic herders they built nothing permanent except their tombs, which fortunately for us they filled with everything they owned in life. The tombs are their monuments.”
 The museum has maintained warm relations with the Hermitage throughout the recent cooling of diplomatic relations between the UK and Russia, and sparked controversy in 2014 when it loaned the St Petersburg institution one of the bitterly contested Parthenon marbles, the first time it had left the UK since Lord Elgin brought them back from Athens in the 19th century. The loans from the Hermitage, and from the National Museum of Kazakhstan, make the exhibition possible about a culture on which the London museum has scant material.
  Written accounts of the Scythians, superb horsemen, brilliant bowmen, and expert craft workers, come from their awed and frequently terrified neighbours. The accounts of Greek historian Herodotus include claims that after drinking their blood the warriors made cloaks of the scalps of their victims in battle. So vivid are the descriptions that Simpson believes Herodotus must have spent time in the Greek settlements on the Black Sea, meeting Scythians and travellers from their territory.
 “It has been questioned whether Herodotus was the first of historians or the first of liars, but increasingly archaeology is proving the accuracy of his accounts.”
 The curator believes one custom explains how the Scythians coped with the savage climate of much of their empire. Herodotus described how they took hemp seeds, threw them on red hot stones in a felt tent, breathed in the vapour, and then “delighted, shout for joy”. Recent excavations have recovered seeds, braziers, tripods and stones exactly as described, and the exhibition will recreate one of the hemp burners – though without the intoxicating fumes.
  Many of the loans reflect the Scythians’ nomadic life style, including an ingenious collapsible table which Simpson described as “proto-Ikea flatpack”. Their herds provided them meat, milk and cheese such as the pieces found in the tomb which could have been made from sheep, goat or mare’s milk, and were intended as food for the afterlife of the warrior.
 Among the loans will be a gold belt buckle showing a group mourning a dead warrior under a tree. His horses also look sorrowful, with good reason: the excavations show their slaughtered steeds accompanied the Scythians to the grave. It came from one of the earliest recorded tomb excavations at the time of Peter the Great, and became the first object in his new museum.
 Another buckle, probably made by a Greek craftsman, shows a bearded warrior with flowing hair, riding bareback, spear in hand. He is wearing soft shoes and long embroidered robes, which Simpson thinks significant: it was their preserved bodies which proved that the Scythian men and women were heavily tattooed, but no outsiders described them. The bodies showed that the tattoos, of abstract patterns or stylised animals, were on chests, upper arms and legs which outsiders would not have seen.
  The tattoo on the skin coming to the exhibition shows part of an tiger with fierce claws. It was found in a tomb at Pazyryk, part of a Unesco world heritage site in the Altai mountains in Siberia, where the contents included the oldest known pile carpet in the world, and the bodies of five heavily tattooed people. Now refrigerated trucks stand by in case human or animal remains are found, such as a chieftain discovered in 2007 dressed in a sable coat.
 When the Pazyryk tombs were excavated in the 1940s, Simpson said, they had no means of preserving the entire bodies and so pragmatically chopped off heads and any particularly interesting body parts.
 The British Museum only has a tiny collection of Scythian related objects, mostly recording their contacts with neighbouring empires. One clay tablet records that the Assyrian king Esarhaddon agreed to send an Assyrian princess to marry the Scythian leader Bartatua in the 7th century BC to seal a peace treaty. “The treaty apparently held, but she swapped life in an Assyrian palace for a nomad’s felt tent – one would so love to have her side of the story, but we hear no more of her,” Simpson said.
title: 大英博物馆将举办Scythian展览(2017)
title_en: British Museum to go more than skin deep with Scythian exhibition (2017)
